blocks.transcript: Ga i — sounds like: gah ee
blocks.notes: Aspirate mutation trigger — P becomes Ph after this

blocks.transcript: mwy o — sounds like: moo-ee oh
blocks.notes: 'o' triggers soft mutation on the following noun

blocks.transcript: datws — sounds like: dat-oos
blocks.notes: Mutated form of tatws — used after 'mwy o'

blocks.transcript: Diolch — sounds like: dee-olch (ch as in loch)
blocks.notes: High frequency. Works as standalone response in any topic.

blocks.transcript: Dim diolch — sounds like: dim dee-olch
os gwelwch yn dda — sounds like: os gwel-ooch un tha
blocks.notes: Formal please. Informal: plîs

blocks.transcript: Dw i'n hoffi — sounds like: doo een HOFF-ee
blocks.notes: Present tense 'I like'. Followed by a noun. South Wales informal.

blocks.transcript: Dw i ddim yn hoffi — sounds like: doo ee thim un HOFF-ee (dd as in 'the')
blocks.notes: Negative of dw i'n hoffi. 'ddim' = not. dd sounds like 'th' in 'the'.

blocks.transcript: Dw i'n llawn — sounds like: doo een thlown (ll = breathy 'thl')
blocks.notes: Standalone phrase. Ll has no English equivalent — tongue to roof of mouth, breathe out the sides.

blocks.transcript: Dw i'n llwgu — sounds like: doo een THLOO-gee
blocks.notes: Literally 'I'm starving/famished'. Everyday hyperbole, kids love it. South Wales usage.

blocks.transcript: Ga i'r — sounds like: gah eer
blocks.notes: 'Ga i' + 'yr' (the) contracted. The r elides before consonants in speech but is written. Use before a definite noun.

blocks.transcript: yn fawr — sounds like: un vowr
blocks.notes: Intensifier. 'Dw i'n hoffi pasta yn fawr' = I like pasta a lot. fawr is mutated form of mawr.

blocks.transcript: plîs — sounds like: pleece
blocks.notes: Informal 'please', borrowed from English, extremely common in spoken South Wales. Formal alternative: os gwelwch yn dda.

blocks.transcript: tatws — sounds like: TAT-oos
blocks.notes: Base (unmutated) form. The mutated form 'datws' is a separate block (block_tatws) used after 'mwy o'.

blocks.transcript: pys — sounds like: pees
blocks.notes: Plural noun, no singular used at table.

blocks.transcript: cig — sounds like: keeg
pasta — sounds like: PAST-a
blocks.notes: Borrowed word — easy win for parents.

blocks.transcript: llaeth — sounds like: thleye-th (ll breathy, th as in 'thin')
blocks.notes: South Wales word for milk. North Wales uses llefrith. Use llaeth throughout.

blocks.transcript: dŵr — sounds like: door
blocks.notes: Circumflex = long vowel.

blocks.transcript: halen — sounds like: HAL-en
Mae'r bwyd — sounds like: mire boo-id
blocks.notes: 'Mae'r' = is the. Sentence opener for stating something about the food.

blocks.transcript: bron yn barod — sounds like: bron un BAR-od
blocks.notes: 'bron' = almost, 'yn barod' = ready.

blocks.transcript: yn barod — sounds like: un BAR-od
swper — sounds like: SOOP-er
blocks.notes: South Wales commonly uses swper for the evening meal. cinio = midday meal. FLAG FOR REVIEW: confirm swper vs te for the family's evening meal in target community.

blocks.transcript: Aros — sounds like: AR-os
blocks.notes: Informal singular command (to one child). Plural/polite: Arhoswch. Parent-to-child register.

blocks.transcript: Eistedda — sounds like: ay-STED-a
blocks.notes: Informal singular command. Plural/polite: Eisteddwch. Parent-to-child.

blocks.transcript: Golcha dy ddwylo — sounds like: GOL-cha duh THWEE-lo
blocks.notes: Informal singular. 'dy' triggers soft mutation: dwylo -> ddwylo. Parent-to-child.

blocks.transcript: Dewch i fwyta — sounds like: dewch ee VOO-uh-ta
blocks.notes: 'i' + bwyta -> fwyta (soft mutation). Dewch is plural/inclusive 'come' — natural when calling the whole family. FLAG FOR REVIEW.

topics.notes: MVP topic. 29 letters. Sneaky sounds: Dd, Ng, Ph use type sound_pattern.

parent_reference.name: A
parent_reference.description: Sounds like 'a' in 'father'
parent_reference.transcript: A — sounds like 'a' in father

words.notes: F in Welsh sounds like English V

parent_reference.name: B
parent_reference.description: Same as English B
parent_reference.transcript: B — same as English B

words.transcript: Bwrdd — sounds like: boorth (rhymes with worth)

words.notes: Borrowed word — easy win for parents

parent_reference.name: C (always hard, like K)
parent_reference.description: Always hard, like K — never like S
parent_reference.transcript: C — always hard like K, never soft like S

parent_reference.name: Ch (like Scottish loch)
parent_reference.description: Like the ch in Scottish 'loch' — a soft rasp at the back of the throat
parent_reference.transcript: Ch — like the ch in Scottish loch

words.notes: Findable under pots and stones in the garden

parent_reference.name: D
parent_reference.description: Same as English D
parent_reference.transcript: D — same as English D

parent_reference.name: Dd (like 'th' in 'the')
parent_reference.description: A sneaky sound — rarely starts a word, hides in the middle
parent_reference.transcript: Dd — like the th in the or this. Never like think.

examples.notes: Starts with Dd — findable in any garden

parent_reference.notes: Sneaky sound. Show Option C copy in UI.

parent_reference.name: E
parent_reference.description: Like 'e' in 'bed'
parent_reference.transcript: E — like e in bed

words.notes: Common in local Welsh place names

parent_reference.name: F (sounds like English V)
parent_reference.description: Sounds like English V — not like English F
parent_reference.transcript: F in Welsh sounds like English V

words.notes: Note: starts with Ff not F — F entry uses mutation examples

parent_reference.notes: F in Welsh = English V sound. All words here are mutated forms.

parent_reference.name: Ff (sounds like English F)
parent_reference.description: Sounds like English F
parent_reference.transcript: Ff — sounds like English F

words.notes: Appears in many local place names
On the kitchen table every day

parent_reference.name: G (always hard)
parent_reference.description: Always hard, like G in 'go' — never soft
parent_reference.transcript: G — always hard like G in go

parent_reference.name: Ng (like 'sing')
parent_reference.description: A sneaky sound — like the ng in sing, hiding in the middle and end of words
parent_reference.transcript: Ng — like the ng at the end of sing

examples.alt: A hand reaching out, representing need
examples.notes: Very high frequency — children hear this constantly

examples.alt: A hand pointing, representing showing something

examples.notes: Hear ng in the middle — myn-ydd

parent_reference.notes: Sneaky sound. Show Option C copy in UI.

parent_reference.name: H
parent_reference.description: Same as English H
parent_reference.transcript: H — same as English H

words.transcript: Haul — sounds like: hile (rhymes with mile)

parent_reference.name: I
parent_reference.description: Like 'ee' in 'see' or 'i' in 'bit'
parent_reference.transcript: I — like ee in see or i in bit

words.transcript: Iawn — sounds like: yown (rhymes with town)

parent_reference.name: J (borrowed sounds)
parent_reference.description: Borrowed into Welsh — same as English J
parent_reference.transcript: J — same as English J, used in borrowed words

parent_reference.name: L
parent_reference.description: Same as English L
parent_reference.transcript: L — same as English L

words.notes: Starts with Ll but listed under L for accessibility

parent_reference.name: Ll (no English equivalent — side of tongue)
parent_reference.description: No English equivalent — place your tongue as if to say L, then breathe out from the sides
parent_reference.transcript: Ll — no English equivalent. Tongue to the roof of the mouth, breathe out from the sides.

parent_reference.name: M
parent_reference.description: Same as English M
parent_reference.transcript: M — same as English M

words.notes: South Wales: always mam not mami

words.alt: An open field or town square
words.notes: In many local Welsh place names

words.transcript: Moch — sounds like: moch (ch as in loch)

parent_reference.name: N
parent_reference.description: Same as English N
parent_reference.transcript: N — same as English N

words.notes: Nos da = good night — high value phrase

parent_reference.name: O
parent_reference.description: Like 'o' in 'hot'
parent_reference.transcript: O — like o in hot

words.transcript: Ochr — sounds like: OCH-r (ch as in loch)
words.alt: An arrow pointing to the side

words.notes: On bikes and toy cars — findable in most homes

parent_reference.name: P
parent_reference.description: Same as English P
parent_reference.transcript: P — same as English P

words.notes: Borrowed word — flower pots in every garden

parent_reference.name: Ph (sounds like F — only appears through mutation)
parent_reference.description: A sneaky sound — Ph is what P becomes after certain words. It always sounds like F.
parent_reference.transcript: Ph sounds like F. It appears when P changes shape after certain words — this is called mutation.

examples.transcript: ei phêl hi — sounds like: ay fail hee
examples.alt: A ball belonging to a girl

examples.transcript: ei phensil hi — sounds like: ay FEN-sil hee
examples.alt: A pencil belonging to a girl
examples.notes: Same mutation pattern — very common in school

examples.transcript: ei phlât hi — sounds like: ay thlat hee
examples.alt: A plate belonging to a girl
examples.notes: Another everyday example of the same mutation

parent_reference.notes: Sneaky sound. Show Option C copy in UI. Ph only appears through mutation — never in base word forms.

parent_reference.name: R (rolled)
parent_reference.description: Rolled — like a Scottish or Spanish R
parent_reference.transcript: R — rolled, like a Scottish or Spanish R

words.notes: Starts with Rh — see Rh entry

parent_reference.name: Rh (breathy R — no English equivalent)
parent_reference.description: A breathy R — no English equivalent. Like R but with a puff of air.
parent_reference.transcript: Rh — a breathy R with no English equivalent

words.transcript: Rhew — sounds like: rhew (breathy r, then ew as in dew)
Rhaw — sounds like: rhow (rhymes with cow)

parent_reference.name: S (always unvoiced — never Z sound)
parent_reference.description: Always the hiss of S — never buzzes like Z
parent_reference.transcript: S — always unvoiced like S in sun, never like Z

words.alt: A star in the night sky

words.notes: Si = sh sound in South Wales

parent_reference.name: T
parent_reference.description: Same as English T
parent_reference.transcript: T — same as English T

words.notes: Circumflex = long vowel. Very high frequency.
The most Welsh of all home objects

parent_reference.name: Th (like 'think' — never voiced)
parent_reference.description: Like 'th' in 'think' — never voiced like 'the'
parent_reference.transcript: Th — like th in think, never like th in the

words.notes: Torth fara (loaf of bread) is very common
Covers both an adjective and a findable object

parent_reference.name: U (South Wales: sounds like I in 'bit')
parent_reference.description: In South Wales, sounds like the I in 'bit'
parent_reference.transcript: U — in South Wales sounds like i in bit

words.notes: Great for play — how high, how loud?
Neidio ar yr unfan — jumping on the spot — school PE word

parent_reference.name: W (vowel — like 'oo' in book)
parent_reference.description: Acts as a vowel — sounds like 'oo' in book
parent_reference.transcript: W — acts as a vowel, sounds like oo in book

words.notes: In every room — great for pointing games

parent_reference.name: Y (two sounds — 'uh' and 'ee')
parent_reference.description: Makes two sounds: 'uh' in the middle of longer words, 'ee' at the end or in short words
parent_reference.transcript: Y — two sounds: uh in the middle of longer words, ee at the end
parent_reference.pronunciation_hint: uh or ee depending on position

words.notes: Ystafell wely (bedroom) is an early school phrase

parent_supplemental.title: Listen and find
parent_supplemental.alternative: Read the letters aloud in order instead
parent_supplemental.notes: Randomly selects a letter from content_refs each session, then pulls that letter's words from its say_together_refs pool so the practice words always match the letter shown.

family_activity.title: Say it together
family_activity.prompt: Everyone say it at the same time. Ready?
family_activity.alternative: Point to something beginning with this letter instead
family_activity.notes: App selects the current letter, then draws words from that letter's say_together_refs (its own examples plus any platform-wide words that genuinely start with the letter, digraph-aware). content_ref set at runtime from that pool.

family_activity.title: Build the word
family_activity.prompt: The letters are all here — put them in the right order to make the Welsh word
family_activity.prompt_welsh: Trefnwch y llythrennau i wneud y gair
family_activity.alternative: Say the word aloud while someone else arranges the letters
family_activity.notes: Easier mechanic — all letters provided including decoys. App selects word from buildable subset.

family_activity.title: Spell it out
family_activity.prompt: Tap the letters one by one to spell the Welsh word
family_activity.prompt_welsh: Tapiwch y llythrennau i sillafu'r gair
family_activity.alternative: Point to each letter in order instead of tapping
family_activity.notes: Harder mechanic — tap from full alphabet. Unlocks after word_builder_arrange completed once.

family_challenge.title: Secret letter
family_challenge.prompt: One person picks a secret letter. Everyone else has to find something in the room beginning with that letter. First to find one wins.
family_challenge.alternative: Point to things instead of naming them — family guesses the letter
family_challenge.notes: No Welsh required. Works on first use.

family_challenge.title: Repeat and beat
family_challenge.prompt: One person says a Welsh word. The next person repeats it and adds one more. How long can the chain get?
family_challenge.alternative: Take turns pointing to things instead — no speaking required
family_challenge.notes: Core play challenge. Memory game mechanic.

family_challenge.title: Letter of the day
family_challenge.prompt: This is today's letter. Every time someone spots something beginning with it — inside or outside — call it out in Welsh.
family_challenge.alternative: Just point when you spot something — no Welsh needed to join in
family_challenge.notes: Ritual mechanic. App selects the letter. Designed to recur daily.

topics.notes: Full topic. Evening family meal at home. Ages 3-5 child, parent as primary learner. Everyday foods, drinks/extras, and likes/dislikes/fullness phrases. Two new mechanics: plate_builder, menu_roleplay. Two new real-world challenges: taste_test, clear_the_table. All Welsh to be verified by a native South Wales speaker before release — see FLAG FOR REVIEW notes. v1.1: added mealtime instruction phrases (almost ready, ready, wait, come and eat, wash hands, sit down) in parent-to-child South Wales register.

parent_reference.transcript: Plât — sounds like: plaht
parent_reference.notes: Circumflex = long vowel

parent_reference.transcript: Cwpan — sounds like: KOO-pan
Fforc — sounds like: fork
parent_reference.notes: Ff sounds like English F

parent_reference.transcript: Llwy — sounds like: thlooee
parent_reference.notes: Ll has no English equivalent — tongue to roof of mouth, breathe out the sides

parent_reference.transcript: Cyllell — sounds like: KUTH-eth (ll breathy both times)
parent_reference.notes: Two Ll sounds. Worth a pronunciation note in UI.

parent_reference.transcript: Bwrdd — sounds like: boorth (rhymes with worth)
parent_reference.alt: A dinner table laid for a meal
parent_reference.notes: Same word as alphabet topic — audio can be shared

parent_reference.transcript: Tatws — sounds like: TAT-oos
parent_reference.notes: Base form. Mutates to datws after mwy o.

parent_reference.transcript: Pys — sounds like: pees
Cig — sounds like: keeg
Pasta — sounds like: PAST-a
parent_reference.notes: Borrowed word — easy win for parents

parent_reference.transcript: Llysiau — sounds like: THLUS-yeye
Caws — sounds like: kowss
Bara — sounds like: BAR-a
parent_reference.notes: Shared with alphabet topic

parent_reference.transcript: Llaeth — sounds like: thleye-th
parent_reference.notes: South Wales word. North Wales: llefrith.

parent_reference.transcript: Dŵr — sounds like: door
parent_reference.notes: Circumflex = long vowel

parent_reference.transcript: Halen — sounds like: HAL-en
Sos — sounds like: sohss
parent_reference.notes: Sos coch = ketchup (literally 'red sauce'), very common with kids

parent_reference.transcript: Ga i fwy o datws — gah ee voo-ee oh dat-oos
parent_reference.alt: A bowl of potatoes being passed across a dinner table
parent_reference.context: Said at the dinner table when you want more food
parent_reference.notes: mwy o triggers soft mutation: tatws becomes datws

parent_reference.transcript: Diolch — dee-olch (ch as in loch)
parent_reference.alt: A smiling person saying thank you
parent_reference.context: Said whenever something is passed or received at the table
parent_reference.notes: High frequency. Same block used across all topics.

parent_reference.welsh_assembled: Ga i'r halen, os gwelwch yn dda
parent_reference.english: Can I have the salt, if you please
parent_reference.english_natural: Can I have the salt please
parent_reference.transcript: Ga i'r halen, os gwelwch yn dda — gah eer HAL-en, os gwel-ooch un tha
parent_reference.pronunciation_hint: gah eer HAL-en, os gwel-ooch un tha
parent_reference.alt: A hand reaching across the table toward a salt shaker
parent_reference.context: Said at the table when you want the salt passed to you
parent_reference.notes: Ga i'r = Ga i + yr (the). FLAG FOR REVIEW: confirm no aspirate mutation needed on halen here.

parent_reference.transcript: Dw i'n hoffi pasta — doo een HOFF-ee PAST-a
parent_reference.alt: A child happily pointing at a bowl of pasta
parent_reference.context: Said to tell someone you like a food
parent_reference.notes: Swap the final block to make a new phrase for any food.

parent_reference.welsh_assembled: Dw i ddim yn hoffi pys
parent_reference.transcript: Dw i ddim yn hoffi pys — doo ee thim un HOFF-ee pees
parent_reference.pronunciation_hint: doo ee thim un HOFF-ee pees
parent_reference.alt: A child pushing a plate of peas away with a playful frown
parent_reference.context: Said to tell someone you don't like a food. Low stakes — meant to be funny, not a real refusal.
parent_reference.notes: The honest first phrase most kids want to say. Keep it light.

parent_reference.transcript: Dw i'n llawn — doo een thlown
parent_reference.alt: A child leaning back from a cleared plate, hand on tummy
parent_reference.context: Said at the end of a meal when you've had enough

parent_reference.transcript: Dw i'n llwgu — doo een THLOO-gee
parent_reference.alt: A child at an empty place setting looking eagerly at the kitchen
parent_reference.context: Said before a meal when you're really hungry
parent_reference.notes: Hyperbole — kids enjoy the drama of it.

parent_reference.english: Can I have the milk, please
parent_reference.english_natural: Can I have the milk please
parent_reference.transcript: Ga i'r llaeth, plîs — gah eer thleye-th, pleece
parent_reference.alt: A jug of milk on a breakfast table
parent_reference.context: Said when you want the milk passed to you
parent_reference.notes: Uses informal plîs — the version a South Wales family actually says.

parent_reference.transcript: Mae'r bwyd bron yn barod — mire boo-id bron un BAR-od
parent_reference.alt: A parent at the cooker glancing at the kids, food nearly done
parent_reference.context: Called out from the kitchen when the meal is nearly done
parent_reference.notes: Parent-to-child everyday usage.

parent_reference.transcript: Mae swper yn barod — meye SOOP-er un BAR-od
parent_reference.alt: A family being called to a set table
parent_reference.context: Called out when the meal is on the table
parent_reference.notes: Uses 'Mae' (not 'Mae'r') before swper — assembled form differs from block label; audio must match welsh_assembled. FLAG FOR REVIEW: swper vs te.

parent_reference.transcript: Aros — AR-os
parent_reference.alt: A parent holding up a hand gently to a child reaching for food
parent_reference.context: Said to a child who's diving in before everyone's ready
parent_reference.notes: Informal singular. Plural: Arhoswch.

parent_reference.english_natural: Come and eat / come to the table
parent_reference.transcript: Dewch i fwyta — dewch ee VOO-uh-ta
parent_reference.alt: A parent waving the family over to the table
parent_reference.context: Said to call everyone to the table

parent_reference.transcript: Golcha dy ddwylo — GOL-cha duh THWEE-lo
parent_reference.alt: A child washing hands at a sink before dinner
parent_reference.context: Said before sitting down to eat
parent_reference.notes: Soft mutation: dwylo -> ddwylo after dy.

parent_reference.transcript: Eistedda — ay-STED-a
parent_reference.alt: A child pulling out a chair at the dinner table
parent_reference.context: Said to get a child into their seat at the table
parent_reference.notes: Informal singular. Plural: Eisteddwch.

parent_supplemental.title: Build the phrase
parent_supplemental.prompt: Tap the blocks in the right order
parent_supplemental.alternative: Read the assembled phrase aloud from the card
parent_supplemental.notes: Shows only unlocked blocks. More blocks = more combinations.

parent_supplemental.title: Listen and find
parent_supplemental.alternative: Read the food words aloud from the cards instead
parent_supplemental.notes: Randomly selects from content_refs each session. Same mechanic as alphabet listen_identify.

family_activity.title: Say it together
family_activity.prompt: Everyone say it at the same time. Ready?
family_activity.alternative: Point to what you want and nod — that counts too

family_activity.title: Build a plate
family_activity.prompt: Drag the food onto the plate. As each one lands, say its name in Welsh together.
family_activity.prompt_welsh: Llusgwch y bwyd ar y plât. Dwedwch yr enw gyda'ch gilydd.
family_activity.alternative: Point to each food on screen and let someone else say the Welsh
family_activity.notes: NEW MECHANIC. Drag-and-drop foods (sourced from content_refs) onto a plate graphic. On drop, play the word audio and show the Welsh label. No score — the plate fills as foods are added. Tablet drag interaction; foods are illustrated word assets reused from parent_reference. Build note: needs a plate SVG container and draggable food tokens. Each token maps to a word_id so audio + label come straight from content.

family_activity.title: Café Cymraeg
family_activity.prompt: One of you is the waiter, one is the customer. The customer orders using 'Ga i...'. The waiter brings it over. Then swap.
family_activity.prompt_welsh: Un yn weinydd, un yn gwsmer. Archebwch gyda 'Ga i...'.
family_activity.alternative: Point to an item on the menu and the waiter names it in Welsh for you
family_activity.notes: NEW MECHANIC. On-screen menu of food cards (drawn from content_refs). The app prompts a role split and shows the 'Ga i...' sentence frame the customer can tap to hear. The waiter taps the ordered item to 'serve' it (plays the word audio). Turn-taking is family-managed, not enforced. Build note: reuses existing word + phrase assets; the only new UI is a menu layout and a serve-tap state. Designed to scale — any topic with food/object words can populate the menu.

family_challenge.title: Phrase of the night
family_challenge.prompt: Tonight's challenge: everyone has to ask for something using Welsh. The app will remind you at dinner time.
family_challenge.alternative: Try just the first word — Ga i is enough to start
family_challenge.notes: Can be presented as a printable card or lock screen reminder

family_challenge.title: Name the table
family_challenge.prompt: Before anyone sits down, take turns naming something on the table in Welsh. How many can you get?
family_challenge.alternative: Point to things instead — family guesses the Welsh word
family_challenge.notes: No Welsh needed to start. Works on first use.

family_challenge.title: Blaswr y nos (Taste tester)
family_challenge.prompt: Pick one taster for tonight. They try a forkful of something on the table and give it a verdict in Welsh: 'Dw i'n hoffi hwn!' or 'Dw i ddim yn hoffi hwn!' Everyone else guesses the verdict before they say it.
family_challenge.alternative: Thumbs up or thumbs down counts as the verdict — no words needed
family_challenge.notes: NEW CHALLENGE. Real-world, screen-down. 'hwn' = this one. Deliberately silly to lower stakes. FLAG FOR REVIEW: confirm 'hwn' is the natural South Wales choice here vs 'hwn 'ma'.

family_challenge.title: Clirio'r bwrdd (Clear the table)
family_challenge.prompt: When the meal's done, everyone clears together — but you can only pick something up after you've named it in Welsh. Last thing on the table loses.
family_challenge.alternative: Name it in English first, then have a go at the Welsh — both count
family_challenge.notes: NEW CHALLENGE. Turns clearing up into the game. 'pre_meal' duration is the closest existing value for an end-of-meal moment; consider adding 'post_meal' to the duration enum later.
